summary: My mother's side of the family, the Borchard family, owned a ranch in Ventura County. They were quite influential in my early life, especially my mother's brothers and sister. Their presence helped shape my upbringing and indirectly influenced my path toward architecture.

Ventura County was home to my mother's family ranch, the Borchard family. Growing up connected to that place gave me a sense of roots outside the urban environment of Los Angeles. It was part of the family background that influenced my early years.

My father, Albert C. Martin, Sr., was an architectural engineer who played a pivotal role in introducing steel and reinforced concrete into building construction. Despite his health challenges, including epilepsy and drinking issues, his work and passion deeply influenced my own career in architecture. I grew up immersed in his world, which gave me practical experience from a young age.

I attended the University of Southern California where I was introduced to the new architectural concepts of the Bauhaus school. My education there was foundational in shaping my architectural philosophy, especially during the challenging times of the Great Depression and World War II. USC provided the academic grounding that complemented my practical experience.

At USC, I learned about the Bauhaus school of architecture, which introduced modernist ideas that influenced my early thinking. However, I eventually preferred an architectural style richer than Bauhaus, incorporating classic design elements and humanism. Bauhaus was an important stepping stone in my development as an architect.

The Great Depression was a difficult period for our architectural firm in Los Angeles. Business struggled, and the economic downturn affected the entire profession. Despite these hardships, we managed to persevere and eventually rebuild, which taught me resilience in both life and work.

World War II had a significant impact on Los Angeles and the architectural profession. During the war years, our firm faced challenges but continued to work and adapt. The post-war period opened new opportunities for growth and innovation in architecture.

Los Angeles has been the central stage for much of my career and life. Our firm designed many landmark buildings here, including the Department of Water and Power Building and the May Company stores. I have also been involved in city planning and community affairs, striving to improve the urban environment and preserve open spaces.

I greatly admired Frank Lloyd Wright and his approach to organic architecture. His influence helped shape my preference for designs that unify structure and environment. Throughout my career, I sought to incorporate his humanistic principles into my work.

Willem Marinus Dudok was another architect I admired for his rich architectural style. His work contrasted with the Bauhaus modernism and inspired me to pursue a more classic and humanistic approach. Dudok's influence is evident in my emphasis on balanced and unified buildings.

entity: Department of Water and Power Building (Los Angeles)
summary: Our firm designed the Department of Water and Power Building in Los Angeles, which was one of our significant projects. This building exemplified our commitment to combining functionality with aesthetic appeal. It stands as a testament to our innovative approach during the mid-20th century.

We worked on designing several May Company stores in Los Angeles, which were important commercial projects for our firm. These stores helped define retail architecture in the region and showcased our ability to blend modern design with practical needs. The May Company projects remain highlights of our portfolio.

The ARCO Towers were among the landmark buildings our firm designed in Los Angeles. These towers represented modern corporate architecture and incorporated advanced engineering techniques. They reflect the evolution of the city's skyline during my career.

We pioneered the design of shopping malls like Lakewood Center, which was one of the first of its kind. This project marked a shift in retail and urban planning, emphasizing open spaces and accessibility. Lakewood Center was a model for future developments in suburban shopping environments.

Eastland Center was another pioneering shopping mall project our firm designed. Like Lakewood Center, it focused on creating open, accessible retail spaces that served the growing suburban population. These projects helped redefine commercial architecture in the mid-20th century.

I was particularly proud to design St. Basil's Church on Wilshire Boulevard in Los Angeles. This project allowed me to blend classic design elements with modern architectural principles. It remains one of the most meaningful works in my career, reflecting both spiritual and aesthetic values.

Wilshire Boulevard is a significant location in Los Angeles where I designed St. Basil's Church. The boulevard itself is a vital urban corridor that has seen many architectural developments. I have also advocated for its renovation to better serve the community and enhance urban life.

I served as chairman of the Los Angeles Bicentennial Committee, where I helped organize events and fundraising efforts. This role allowed me to contribute to the city's cultural life and promote community involvement. My brother Ed and I were both active in city planning and civic affairs through this and other initiatives.
